coredata.dc:title: A label-free ECL aptasensor for sensitive detection of carcinoembryonic antigen based on CdS QDs@MOF and TEOA@Au as bi-coreactants of Ru(bpy)3 2+
coredata.dc:creator: Wei, Xiu-hua
Qiao, Xing
Fan, Jie
Hao, Yuan-qiang
Zhang, Yin-tang
Zhou, Yan-li
Xu, Mao-tian
coredata.dc:description: Herein, a label-free electrochemiluminescence (ECL) biosensor for sensitive determination of carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA) was fabricated based on CdS quantum dots loaded onto metal organic framework (CdS QDs@MOF) and triethanolamine modified on gold nanoparticles (TEOA@AuNPs) as bi-coreactants of Ru(bpy)3 2+ ECL system. The TEOA@AuNPs served as not only coreactant of Ru(bpy)3 2+ but also nanocarrier for CEA aptamer which was bonded to the TEOA@AuNPs through Au–S bond. After incubated in CEA solution, the ECL signals decreased due to the poor conductivity of CEA and there was a linear correlation between the ECL intensity and log(CCEA). Based on this result, a label-free ECL biosensor has been developed successfully and used to detect CEA with a detection limit of 8.5 × 10−5 ng/mL. The proposed ECL aptasensor had good analytical performance in the detection of CEA in human serum samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Electrochemiluminescence
Aptasensor
Carcinoembryonic antigen
CdS quantum dots
Triethanolamine
Bi-coreactants

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of CdS QDs and CdS QDs@MOF
Fabrication and characterization of ECL aptasensor

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Young Key Teacher Project of Universities in Henan Province
National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant Nos. 21505091 and 22074089 ) and Young Key Teacher Project of Universities in Henan Province (Grant No. 2018GGJS133 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) High-resolution TEM image of CdS QDs (the inset is the magnified TEM image of an individual CdS QD). SEM image (B), high-resolution TEM image (C) and XPS survey spectra (D) of CdS QDs@MOF composite. TEM image (E) and UV–vis absorption spectra (F) of TEOA@Au NPs.

(A) The ECL signals on different modified electrodes in 0.1 M PBS (pH = 7.4) containing 0.1 mM Ru(bpy)3 2+. EIS (B) and cyclic voltammetry curves (C) of bare GCE(a), CdSQDs@MOF/GCE(b), TEOA@Au/CdSQDs@MOF/GCE(c), Apt/TEOA@Au/CdS QDs@MOF/GCE(d), β-ME/Apt/TEOA@Au/CdS QDs@MOF/GCE(e) and CEA/β-ME/Apt/TEOA@Au/CdS QDs@MOF/GCE (f) in 5.0 mM [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− containing 0.1 M KCl. Insets in B were the scheme of the equivalent circuit and the EIS of GCE respectively.

The effects of the CdS QDs@MOF concentration (A), the drip dose of CdS QDs@MOF suspension (B), the drip dose of TEOA@Au suspension (C) and the incubation time in 100 fg/ml CEA solution (D) on ECL intensity in 0.1 M PBS (pH = 7.4) containing 0.1 mM Ru(bpy)3 2+.
(A) ECL response of the aptasensor to CEA concentration (a-h:100 fg/mL-10 ng/mL). (B) calibration curve for CEA detection in 0.1 M PBS (pH = 7.4) containing 0.1 mM Ru(bpy)3 2+.

(A) Reproducibility of the ECL signal collected from four different electrodes (CCEA = 100 pg/mL);(B) Selectivity of the proposed ECL aptamer sensor with 10 ng/mL BSA, Thrombin, Lysozyme, Trypsin and 100 pg/mL CEA.
Fabrication procedure of the proposed aptasensor.
Comparison of analytical performance of different sensors for the detection of CEA.
Recovery tests for CEA in human serum samples with the proposed aptasensor.
Comparison of CEA concentrations detected by two methods.

head.ce:title: A label-free ECL aptasensor for sensitive detection of carcinoembryonic antigen based on CdS QDs@MOF and TEOA@Au as bi-coreactants of Ru(bpy)3 2+

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Henan Key Laboratory of Biomolecular Recognition and Sensing, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Henan Joint International Research Laboratory of Chemo/Biosensing and Early Diagnosis of Major Diseases, Shangqiu Normal University, Shangqiu 476000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Henan Key Laboratory of Biomolecular Recognition and Sensing
College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering
Henan Joint International Research Laboratory of Chemo/Biosensing and Early Diagnosis of Major Diseases

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Henan Key Laboratory of Biomolecular Recognition and Sensing, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Henan Joint International Research Laboratory of Chemo/Biosensing and Early Diagnosis of Major Diseases, Shangqiu Normal University, Shangqiu 476000, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Central Lab at Zhenzhou First People Hospital, Zhengzhou First People Hospital, Zhengzhou 450000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Central Lab at Zhenzhou First People Hospital

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Central Lab at Zhenzhou First People Hospital, Zhengzhou First People Hospital, Zhengzhou 450000, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, a label-free electrochemiluminescence (ECL) biosensor for sensitive determination of carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA) was fabricated based on CdS quantum dots loaded onto metal organic framework (CdS QDs@MOF) and triethanolamine modified on gold nanoparticles (TEOA@AuNPs) as bi-coreactants of Ru(bpy)3 2+ ECL system. The TEOA@AuNPs served as not only coreactant of Ru(bpy)3 2+ but also nanocarrier for CEA aptamer which was bonded to the TEOA@AuNPs through Au–S bond. After incubated in CEA solution, the ECL signals decreased due to the poor conductivity of CEA and there was a linear correlation between the ECL intensity and log(CCEA). Based on this result, a label-free ECL biosensor has been developed successfully and used to detect CEA with a detection limit of 8.5 × 10−5 ng/mL. The proposed ECL aptasensor had good analytical performance in the detection of CEA in human serum samples.

ce:section.ce:para: Recently, ECL has attracted wide attention due to its advantages of high sensitivity, wide linear range, good reproducibility, easy control and some reagents can be reused [1,2]. It has been widely used in environmental monitoring, food analysis and clinical diagnosis [1,3]. Ru(bpy)3 2+ is the most commonly used luminescent material because of the good stability, electrochemical reversibility, reproducible excitation and high luminous efficiency [4,5]. Coreactants are often used in Ru(bpy)3 2+ luminescence systems to increase ECL efficiency. Commonly used coreactants are tripropylamine and 2-(dibutylamino)-ethanol, but they are highly toxic and volatile [6]. In recent years, many new coreactants such as polyethyleneimine (PEI) [7], polyamidoamine [8], quantum dots [9] and carbon dots [10] have been developed. triethanolamine (TEOA) has also become a more efficient co-reactant due to its low toxicity, good water solubility and environmental friendliness [11–13]. Simultaneously, bi-coreactants have been synthesized and attempted to improve the ECL intensity. For examples, polyamidoamine functionalized AuNPs combined with graphene oxide loaded with PEI to enhance the ECL signal of Ru(bpy)3 2+ synergistically [14]. PEI capped N-doped carbon dots [15] and PEI capped CdS quantum dots [16] have been synthesized and served as bi-coreactant.

Studies have shown that the anodic electrochemical behavior of QDs is similar to that of tripropylamine, so QDs can act as co-reactants for Ru(bpy)3 2+ [9]. Fixing QDs directly on the electrode has problems such as unstable signal and low efficiency. The metal organic skeleton (MOF) is a microporous material with a large specific surface area, regular shape and pore size, and large internal pores, which can be used to fix other nanomaterials [17–20]. Thus, MOF can be used as a carrier of quantum dots, thereby improving the utilization of quantum dots.

Carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA) is a tumor marker for different types of cancer, such as liver cancer, colon cancer and breast cancer. Various ECL strategies have been applied in CEA detection. One of the most commonly used is the label-free ECL sensor because of its advantages such as no labeling, strong specificity and fast analysis speed [21–24]. However, the application of the bi-coreactants ECL strategy to the detection of CEA has not been reported.

In this work, TEOA@AuNPs and CdS QDs@MOF were prepared and used as bi-coreactants of Ru(bpy)3 2+ for the first time to synergistically amplification of the ECL signal. TEOA@AuNPs was used not only as coreactant but also as nanocarrier for CEA aptamer via Au-S bonds. In addition, after CEA was bound to its aptamer, the ECL signal was weakened, which was due to the increased impedance between Ru(bpy)3 2+ and the bi-coreactants. Based on this ECL system, a highly sensitive label-free ECL biosensor for CEA analysis has been constructed successfully and exhibited good analytical performance. The satisfactory results were achieved in determination of CEA in human serum samples.

Biphenyl-4,4′-dicarboxylic acid (BDC), Zn(NO3)2·6H2O, Cd(NO3)2·4H2O, polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP) and nafion (5 wt%) were purchased from aladdin (Shanghai, China). Triethanolamine (TEOA), β-mercaptoethanol (β-ME), N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF), Ru(bpy)3Cl2·6H2O, HAuCl4, Na2S·9H2O and mercaptopropionic acid (MPA) were purchased from XiYa Reagent (Shandong, China). Carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA) and aptamer for CEA were obtained from China Peptides Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). The aptamer squence is 5′-SH-AGGGGGAAGGGATACCC-3′. Ultrapure water with a resistivity of 18.2 MΩ.cm and phosphate buffer solution (PBS, 0.1 M, pH = 7.4) containing 0.1 M NaH2PO4, 0.1 M Na2HPO4 and 0.05 M KCl were used throughout this research.

ECL measurements were carried out with an MPI-A/B ECL analyzer (Xi’an Remax Electronic Science & Technology Co., Ltd., China), in which the voltage of the photomultiplier tube was set at 800 V. The potential scanning range was 0 ∼ 1.5 V and the scan rate was 100 mV s−1. Cyclic voltammetric (CV) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measurements were carried out with a CHI 660D electrochemistry workstation (CH Instruments, Shanghai, China). All experiments were performed by a traditional three-electrode system. Ag/AgCl electrode, glassy carbon electrode (GCE, Φ = 3 mm) and a Pt wire served as reference electrode, working electrode and counter electrode respectively. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM, S-4800, Hitachi, Japanese) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM, H-800, Hitachi, Japanese) were used to investigate sample morphology. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) were measured with an ES-CALAB 250 XPS (Thermoltd, USA).

TEOA@Au was synthesized according to the reported literature [13]. 2 mL of TEOA (0.1 M) was added to 35 mL ultrapure water at 100 °C under vigorous stirred. Next 200 μL of 42.8 mM HAuCl4 was added drop by drop. Then the mixed solution was reacted under reflux for 50 min. The solution color changed to wine red, suggesting that TEOA@Au NPs was prepared successfully.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of CdS QDs and CdS QDs@MOF
ce:section.ce:para: CdS QDs was prepared according to the reported methods with a little modification [25]. First, the 20 mL Cd(NO3)2·4H2O (0.1 M) solution was slowly dropped into the 5.0 mL of MPA (1.0 M) solution under continuous stirring. Then adjust the solution pH to 6.0–7.0 with 0.5 M NaOH solution. Last, added 10 mL of 0.1 M Na2S solution, the solution color turned clear and then pale yellow, indicating a successful preparation.

CdS QDs@MOF was prepared according to the methods reported in the literature with a slight change [26]. Under stirring, 1 mL of the prepared CdS QDs was dripped into the mixed solution containing 0.1 g PVP, 9 mL DMF and 5 mL ethanol. Subsequently, 4.5 mg of Zn(NO3)2·6H2O and 8.3 mg of BDC dissolved in 3 mL DMF were added to the obtained solution, followed by ultrasonication for 30 min. Then transferred the mixed solution to a autoclave and kept heating for 20 h at 100 °C. After the reaction was completed, the obtained product was collected by centrifuging and washing with DMF (8,000 rpm, 5 min) for three times. Finally CdS@MOF was dried at 80 °C and dispersed into 0.2% nafion solution, the concentration of the CdS@MOF was 6 mg/mL.

All glassy carbon electrodes were polished before modification. The treatment process was as follows: after polishing with 0.3 and 0.05 μm alumina slurry, rinsed with deionized water and then ultrasonicated in ultrapure water and ethanol in turn. The cleaned electrodes were blown dry with nitrogen. As shown in Scheme 1 , the first step in the construction process, 5 µL CdS QDs@MOF and 6 µL TEOA@Au was dripped onto the GCE surface successively and dried in the air. secondly, the electrode was immersed into 1 µM thiol-functionalized Aptamer and incubated at 4 °C for 7 h. Thirdly, the electrode was blocked by 0.1 mM β-ME for 30 min to eliminate the nonspecific binding sites and washed with PBS (pH = 7.4). Then, the electrode was immersed in CEA solutions of different concentrations and incubated for 90 min at 37 °C. After rinsing with PBS (pH = 7.4), the electrode was stored at 4 °C for later use.

The morphology of the as-prepared nanomaterials was characterized by SEM and TEM techniques. As displayed in Fig. 1 A, the as-synthesized CdS QDs of about 4 nm were in a monodisperse state and the clear lattice fringes indicating high crystallinity were observed obviously. As can be seen from the inset in Fig. 1A, the single CdS QD exhibited the interplanar spacing of 0.35 nm which was attributed to the (002) plane of CdS QDs [27], indicating CdS QDs were successfully synthesized.

As can be seen from Fig. 1B, the CdS@MOF displayed a flower-like spherical morphology with a particle size of about 100 nm. The well-resolved lattice planes were clearly observed from the high-resolution TEM image (Fig. 1C) of CdS@MOF. The interplanar spacing of 0.35 nm exhibited in Fig. 1C was consistent with the interplanar spacing exhibited in Fig. 1A, indicating what decorated with the MOF materials was CdS QDs. Meanwhile, the elemental composition of CdS QDs@MOF was characterized with XPS. As displayed in Fig. 1D, the characteristic peaks of C1s, O1s, N1s, Zn2p, S2p and Cd3d of CdS QDs@MOF were all appeared, which further conformed the MOF materials has been successfully assembled with CdS QDs.

The Au NPs prepared by TEOA were characterized with TEM and UV − vis absorption spectra. As displayed in Fig. 1E, the as-prepared Au NPs were almost 20 nm spherical. UV–vis absorption spectra (Fig. 1F) showed an absorption peak appeared at about 520 nm, which was the typical absorption peak of Au NPs [28].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication and characterization of ECL aptasensor
ce:section.ce:para: Various aptasensors have been constructed based on the specific recognition between the aptamer and the target. Herein, an ECL aptasensor for CEA determination was proposed and the fabrication process displayed in Scheme 1 was confirmed by recording ECL response of different modified electrodes. As shown in Fig. 2 A, the ECL signal of Ru(bpy)3 2+ at the bare GCE was very weak without any coreactant (curve a). However, the ECL signal increased greatly at the GCE modified with CdS QDs@MOF nanocomposites (curve b). The reason was that CdS QDs could serve as coreactant of Ru(bpy)3 2+ ECL system [16] and the MOF materials has a large specific surface area to facilitate carrying more CdS QDs. After CdS QDs@MOF nanocomposites, TEOA@Au NPs were assembled on the electrode surface (curve c), resulting in a further increase in ECL intensity. The TEOA on TEOA@Au NPs was also used as coreactant of Ru(bpy)3 2+ [13] and the Au NPs provided a nanocarrier for aptamer to form label-free aptasensor. In this ECL system, CdS QDs@MOF nanocomposites and TEOA@Au NPs as bi-coreactant can synergistically improve the ECL intensity of Ru(bpy)3 2+. After aptamer and β-ME was bonded onto the TEOA@Au NPs in sequence (curve d in Fig. 2A), the ECL intensity was further reduced, which could be due to the insulation of the aptamer and β-ME. When CEA was bonded to its aptamer, the ECL signal was further weakened (curve e in Fig. 2A), which was attributed to the poor conductivity of CEA. Thus, an ECL aptasensor has been proposed and fabricated for CEA detection.

The fabrication process of sensors is often characterized by EIS. In a typical Nyquist plot of EIS, the semicircle diameter represents the resistance of interfacial charge transfer (Rct). Herein, EIS was employed to further characterize the fabrication process of the proposed ECL aptasensor and the Rct was obtained by fitting EIS experimental results to an equivalent circuit model (inset in Fig. 2B). As shown in Fig. 2B, the Rct of CdS QDs@MOF/GCE (1051 Ω, curve b) increased significantly compared with the bare GCE (56 Ω, curve a), indicating that CdS QDs@MOF can significantly impede electron transfer. However, the Rct decreased after modified with TEOA@Au NPs (836 Ω, curve c), which could be attributed to the good conductivity of TEOA@Au NPs. After the TEOA@Au/CdS QDs@MOF/GCE was incubated in Apt (1199 Ω, curve d), β-ME (1489 Ω, curve e) and CEA (1721 Ω, curve f) in sequence, the Rct also increased sequentially due to the poor conductivity and steric hindrance [29]. All the above conclusions were consistent with the results of cyclic voltammetry in Fig. 2C, indicating that the proposed ECL biosensor was fabricated successfully.

Since CdS QDs@MOF served as coreactant, the ECL intensity of Ru(bpy)3 2+ ECL should depend on the amount of CdS QDs@MOF modified on the GCE. The concentration and drip dose of CdS QDs@MOF suspension were optimized. As displayed in Fig. 3 A and Fig. 3B, the ECL signal of the GCE electrode modified with CdS QDs@MOF reached a maximum respectively when the concentration was 6 mg/mL and the drip dose was 5 µL. To obtain the strongest ECL intensity, 5 µL, 6 mg/mL CdS QDs@MOF was chosen to modify the GCE. In this ECL system, the TEOA@Au NPs acted as not only coreactant of Ru(bpy)3 2+ but also nanocarrier for the ECL bioassay. For this reason, the amount of TEOA@Au modified on the electrode was also optimized. As can be seen from Fig. 3C, the ECL intensity reached a maximum at 6 µL. Therefore, 6 µL of TEOA@Au was chosen in the construction of ECL biosensor. The accuracy of the biosensor depended on CEA incubation time partly, so the incubation times of the proposed ECL sensor in 100 fg/ml CEA solution were optimized. As displayed in Fig. 3D, the ΔECL intensity increased with the increasing of CEA incubation time, indicating more CEA was captured onto the sensor surface by the aptamer. The stable ΔECL intensity can be obtained at the CEA incubation time of 90 min, so 90 min incubation time was chosen in the following experiments.

The analytical performances of the proposed aptasensor was studied under the above-mentioned optimal conditions. As displayed in Fig. 4 A, the ECL intensity decreased gradually with the increase of CEA concentration from 100 fg/mL to 10 ng/mL. A good linear regression of ECL signal upon logCCEA was established (Fig. 4B), with a regression equation of I = -161log[CCEA/ng.mL−1] + 907 (R2 = 0.994). This developed ECL biosensor exhibited a wider linear range of 0.0001–10 ng/mL and the detection limit of 8.5 × 10−5 ng/mL (S/N = 3) was lower than the previously reported sensors listed in Table 1 . This comparison highlighted the advantage of this bi-coreactants ECL strategy for CEA detection and indicated the proposed ECL sensor could have vast application prospects in ultrasensitive detection of CEA in real sample.

The reproducibility of the developed ECL sensor was explored with the same electrode and with the four different electrodes respectively. As shown in Fig. 5 A, the relative standard deviation was 4.5% for the four different electrodes and 3.1% for three dependent measurements carried on the same electrode, indicating the ECL sensor exhibits good reproducibility in CEA determination.

The inset in Fig. 5B was a real map of ECL signals of the proposed biosensor under continuous cyclic voltammetry scanning for 600 s, which demonstrated the excellent stability of the proposed ECL biosensor. In addition, the long-term stability of the biosensor was examined. After a storage of one week at 4℃, the ECL biosensor maintained 92.3% signal response, suggesting the developed biosensor had good stability.

The selectivity of the developed ECL biosensor for CEA determination was evaluated. The changes of ECL signal were measured when the ECL biosensors were incubated in CEA and other interferences such as BSA, Thrombin, Lysozyme and Trypsin. As shown in Fig. 5B, when the concentration of the four interferences was 100-fold higher than CEA, the ΔECL responses of the four interferences were all less than 10% of that of CEA, which indicating the proposed biosensor had excellent selectivity for CEA.

The CdS QDs@MOF and TEOA@Au nanocomposites were successfully synthesized respectively. As bi-coreactants, CdS QDs@MOF and TEOA@Au modified on GCE have a synergistic effect on enhancing ECL signal of Ru(bpy)3 2+ in the neutral buffer solution without other coreactants. The specific binding of target CEA toward its aptamer could reduce the conductivity of the modified electrode surface, thereby reducing the ECL signal. The ECL signal was linearly related to common logarithm of CEA concentration. As a result, a Label-free ECL aptasensor based on CdS QDs@MOF, TEOA@Au and CEA aptamer has been constructed successfully. The developed ECL aptasensor presented good analytical performance in CEA analysis in human serum samples.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant Nos. 21505091 and 22074089) and Young Key Teacher Project of Universities in Henan Province (Grant No. 2018GGJS133).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Triethanolamine-modified gold nanoparticles synthesized by a one-pot method and their application in electrochemiluminescent immunoassy
